items.title: Provide a generic CatalogCrudService<T> for master data
items.description: Master and catalog entities should be served by one generic CatalogCrudService<T> exposing Create, Update, Deactivate, List and Get. Centralising CRUD in a single typed service keeps validation, soft-delete handling and list filtering consistent across every catalog entity instead of being re-implemented per controller.
items.applicability: Catalog and master-data entities (items, accounts, customers, suppliers, warehouses) of a generated ERP that need standard maintenance screens.
items.example: CatalogCrudService<Item> is registered once; the Item controller calls service.List(), service.Get(id), service.Create(model), service.Update(model) and service.Deactivate(id) without bespoke data code.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: constrain T to a shared base (an identifiable, soft-deletable entity) so the generic operations are type-safe. Test implication: exercise the five operations once generically and once per concrete entity. Generic CRUD fits master data only; it deliberately offers no posting, approval or balanced-voucher logic.

items.title: Deactivate with a soft-delete flag, never hard delete audited data
items.description: Deactivate must set an IsDeleted flag (and a deactivation stamp) rather than issue a SQL DELETE. Audited master data is referenced by historical documents, so physically removing a row would orphan postings and destroy the audit trail. Soft-delete preserves referential history while hiding the record from active use.
items.applicability: Every catalog entity whose primary key is referenced by transactional or audited documents in a generated ERP.
items.example: Deactivate(customerId) sets IsDeleted=true; a sales invoice posted last quarter still resolves its customer, but the customer no longer appears in new-invoice pickers.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: every read path that should hide deleted rows must filter IsDeleted=false, ideally via a global query filter. Test implication: assert Deactivate leaves the row present with IsDeleted=true and that dependent documents still resolve it. Soft-delete grows the table over time and does not satisfy a hard data-erasure requirement, which needs a separate, governed purge process.

items.title: Exclude soft-deleted rows from list queries by default
items.description: List operations must exclude IsDeleted rows by default so deactivated records do not clutter active screens or pickers. The exclusion belongs in the data layer (a global query filter or an explicit Where(IsDeleted==false)) so no caller can forget it; an explicit opt-in is required to view deactivated rows for audit.
items.applicability: List, search and lookup endpoints for every soft-deletable catalog entity in a generated ERP.
items.example: CatalogCrudService.List() returns only active items; an admin 'show deactivated' toggle calls a separate path that ignores the filter for review purposes.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: apply the filter centrally (global query filter) rather than per query so it cannot be omitted. Test implication: create then deactivate a row and assert List() omits it while the audit view includes it. A global filter also hides deleted rows from joins, so deliberate audit reads must explicitly IgnoreQueryFilters.

items.title: Build create and edit forms by reflecting over the entity
items.description: Create and edit forms can be generated by reflecting over the entity's public properties to emit a field per column, choosing an input type from the CLR type and honouring data annotations. This yields consistent maintenance screens for every catalog entity without hand-writing a view per type.
items.applicability: Master-data maintenance screens of a generated ERP where many catalog entities share a uniform form layout.
items.example: The edit view enumerates Item's properties, renders a text box for Name, a number box for a decimal Rate, and a checkbox for a bool flag, skipping keys and audit columns.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: exclude system columns (Id, IsDeleted, audit stamps) and map each CLR type deliberately so binding is correct. Test implication: assert the rendered form contains an input for each editable property. Reflection-driven forms suit flat master data; complex layouts, child grids and conditional fields need bespoke views.

items.title: Make master data mutable but keep posted documents immutable
items.description: Catalog and master records may be edited in place, but a document that has been posted to the ledger must be immutable. Posted documents change state only through submit, approve, cancel and reverse actions; correcting one means cancelling or reversing and re-entering, never editing the original. This protects the integrity of historical postings.
items.applicability: Any generated ERP distinguishing editable master data from posted transactional documents.
items.example: An item's description is edited freely; a submitted sales invoice cannot be edited — an error correction is made by cancelling it and raising a corrected one, preserving the original for audit.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: gate edit endpoints on document status so a posted document rejects Update. Test implication: assert editing a submitted document is refused and that a reversal path exists. The rule defines lifecycle, not who may act; the actual permission to cancel or reverse is governed separately by RBAC.

items.title: Map empty form fields to empty string for non-nullable string columns
items.description: When binding a form, an empty input for a non-nullable string column must bind to an empty string, and only a nullable value type may bind to null. A real NOT-NULL violation (an HTTP 500) occurred this sprint when an empty optional string field bound to null against a NOT-NULL column; the fix coerces empty strings to '' and reserves null for genuinely nullable value-type fields.
items.applicability: Form binding for every reflection-driven or hand-written create/edit screen in a generated ERP.
items.example: An optional Notes field left blank binds as '' for a non-nullable nvarchar column; a blank optional decimal Discount field binds as null because its column is decimal? (nullable).
items.limitation: Implementation implication: apply the coercion in the binding layer keyed on the column's nullability, not ad hoc per field. Test implication: submit a form with every optional field blank and assert no NOT-NULL violation occurs. The rule prevents the persistence error; it does not decide whether '' or a default is the semantically correct value, which is a per-field business choice.

items.title: Surface required-Name validation in the UI
items.description: Catalog entities require a non-empty Name, and that requirement must be enforced server-side and shown to the user as an inline validation message, not just rejected with a raw error. The create/edit form binds validation state so a blank Name highlights the field and explains the requirement.
items.applicability: Create and edit screens of every named catalog entity in a generated ERP.
items.example: Submitting a new warehouse with a blank Name redisplays the form with 'Name is required' beside the field and a 400/redisplay, not a 500 or a silently saved blank record.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: validate on the server (data annotation plus model-state check) so a client bypass cannot save a blank Name. Test implication: post a blank Name and assert the validation message is returned and nothing is persisted. UI validation improves usability but the server check is the authority; client-only validation must never be trusted.

items.title: Emit stable data-testid hooks for Playwright
items.description: Generated CRUD screens must carry stable, semantic data-testid attributes so end-to-end tests select elements by intent rather than brittle CSS or text. Use predictable names: record-table for the list, edit-form for the form, edit-{id} for a row's edit control and deactivate-{id} for its deactivate control.
items.applicability: Every generated maintenance screen of an ERP that ships Playwright end-to-end coverage.
items.example: A Playwright test does page.getByTestId('edit-42').click(), fills page.getByTestId('edit-form'), submits, then page.getByTestId('deactivate-42').click() to verify the full lifecycle.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: derive testids deterministically from the entity and row id so they are stable across renders. Test implication: assert each generated screen exposes the documented testids. Test hooks aid automation only; they must carry no behaviour and must not leak sensitive identifiers into the DOM.

items.title: Generate per-entity create, edit and deactivate tests
items.description: For each catalog entity the generator should emit tests covering the create, edit and deactivate flows end to end: create a record and assert it appears in the list, edit a field and assert the change persists, deactivate it and assert it disappears from the active list while remaining for audit.
items.applicability: CI coverage for the generated CRUD surface of every catalog entity in an ERP.
items.example: A WarehouseCrudTests class creates a warehouse via the form, edits its address, deactivates it, and asserts each step against the record-table and the data layer.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: drive the tests through the same service or UI path users take, not direct database writes, so binding bugs are caught. Test implication: each entity gets its own create/edit/deactivate case. These prove CRUD mechanics per entity; they do not validate cross-entity workflows, which belong to scenario-level tests.

items.title: Return the soft-deleted row's identity from Deactivate for verification
items.description: Deactivate should report success in a way a caller or test can verify — confirming the row now has IsDeleted=true rather than returning a bare void. This lets the UI show an accurate confirmation and lets tests assert the soft-delete actually took effect instead of assuming it did.
items.applicability: The Deactivate operation of CatalogCrudService<T> and the controllers that surface it in a generated ERP.
items.example: Deactivate(id) loads the entity, sets IsDeleted=true, saves, and returns the updated entity (or a confirmation), so the controller can render 'Customer deactivated' and a test can assert the flag.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: re-read or return the persisted state so the confirmation reflects the database, not the pre-save model. Test implication: assert the returned state has IsDeleted=true and the active list no longer contains it. Returning the entity must not leak large or sensitive columns the caller does not need; project to what the confirmation requires.

items.title: Scope generic reflection CRUD to master data, not transactional documents
items.description: Generic reflection-driven CRUD is the right tool for master and catalog data, but it is not a substitute for the bespoke screens transactional documents need. Posted documents require balanced-voucher entry, line grids, submit/approve workflows and immutability that a generic property-by-property form cannot express, so those screens are purpose-built.
items.applicability: Deciding, during generation, which entities get generic CRUD and which get hand-authored document screens in an ERP.
items.example: Items, accounts and warehouses use the generic CRUD service and reflected forms; a sales invoice with multiple lines, taxes and a submit workflow gets a dedicated controller and view.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: classify each entity as master or transactional during generation and route it to the correct UI strategy. Test implication: assert transactional documents are not exposed through the generic edit endpoint. The boundary is a design choice; mis-classifying a transactional entity as master data would let it be edited in violation of posting immutability.
